title: FCTA - Physics I - May 19
text:
Status Update
We had the first three hour monthly planning meeting. Somewhat light attendance but I think a lot was discussed. 

    We talked about the big picture in terms of what we're aiming for in the Fall and Spring 2026
    We talked about incorporating some of the metacognitive work that Shawn Miller and Michelle Waley have done for Orgo and Chem for Physics. 
    We talked about the different strands and what the goals and objectives are for each portion
        Assessment Strand
            Draft of second exam
            First rubrics to NDL for creation of fake data set
        Weekly Material 
            Demo list
            Identify any new demos
            Powerpoint template with ODL
            Try with 5 first class sessions
            Start exchange and create lecture slides process (according to reasonable summer schedules)
        Course
            Initial Course Schedule
            Draft syllabus
            Draft list of roles and responsibilities
        Videos
            Script or equivalent for 5 metacognition videos
                How to read a physics textbook
                Problem-solving in physics, part 1: setting up and finding algebraic solutions
                Problem-solving in physics, part 2: selecting your strategy 
                How to study for a physics exam
                Functional reasoning
            Meet with video team to discuss plans/scripts for each video to ensure feasibility and possibly start animation creation

text:
We had a great session on 12/9 working in the course map and the team committed to making some more significant progress before our meeting on 12/16. They have already begun doing so, but over break it will be paramount that they dig into the available videos they mapped to ensure their viability ahead of upcoming media discussions. Again, at this point I believe, and one of the physicists confirmed, that any new videos produced will likely be more geared towards study skills, metacognition, etc. However, some more discussion needs to be had about how these fit into the overall course map/ecosystem. 

We discussed this and some other project needs involving the learning designer role with Kristi and Kathy on 12/11 in preparation for my leave.

author: Brianna Stines
title: Status update - Nov 26
text:
Summary
On M 11/25 we discussed 4.5/15 weeks of instructional materials on the course map considering both text/online platforms. After doing so, the group is heavily leaning towards a textbook (Randall Knight’s https://www.pearson.com/en-us/subject-catalog/p/physics-for-scientists-and-engineers-a-strategic-approach-with-modern-physics/P200000006998/9780137344826?tab=table-of-contents published by Pearson). Thus, they'll use https://www.pearson.com/en-us/higher-education/products-services/mastering/physics.html as the online homework platform, resource center, etc. Dare I say, they are decided!? They are refining the week they did and completing another by our 12/9 meeting. So, by then we'll have more than half of the course map about 3/4 baked. Some video ideas did bubble up (e.g., reading strategies for STEM texts) but I do not anticipate them wanting to create new videos for every session/every week with the amount of videos available from Pearson.

On M 12/2 We'll be hearing from some other folks on campus about how they've incorporated additional help sessions into their large STEM course and tackle soem big questions around quiz logistics.

Next steps
In addition to the meeting with NEEL on 3/10, we are meeting with Sugana Chawla this Friday about preparing the iTREDS Scholars to serve as NEEL Teaching Fellows.

iTREDS Scholars Program: The newly launched Interdisciplinary Traineeship for Socially Responsible and Engaged Data Scientists (iTREDS) Scholars Program trains undergraduate students at the University of Notre Dame and Saint Mary’s College in data science through a lens of social responsibility and community engagement, including rigor and responsibility, ethics, society, and policy.

author: Michael Lerma
title: AR Math Visualizer (Summer 2025 Pilot) - Jun 22
text:
Summary

We had a demo with Adamari for the vector field portion she was working on and generally, the feedback was positive. 

From the student feedback - 
    Students liked it
    However, aesthetic things were problematic - choice of color for the planes - made it unclear what was going on - all planes being blue for example
    Some glitches 
    Hard to move the simulation around
    Kind of three types of people - I liked it, I didn’t like it, I couldn’t tell what was going on

We also talked about adding a couple of survey questions for the students so we can understand what phone they're using and pinpoint any problems. We also thought an open ended question would be good for additional feedback.

For Brian's next class Adamari is going to provide some pre-created QR codes to make it easier for the class and to make sure the functionality is correct. 

----------------------------------------

text:
Summary
I'm keeping this at risk as we still search for solutions for the issues.

The good news:
    All of the UI/UX for the website is working 
    Adamari will be adding in all of the definitions that Brian provided
    This will be considered V1 of the Math Visualizer

The bad news:
    The load times for some of the functionality still remains long and unusable at the moment

The hopeful news:
    We talked with Paul, the main PI from CalcPlot3D and he had some suggestions for Adamari to consider 
    This has led her to posting on the 8th wall forums and there is a possibility of using a legacy editor that may allows us to bring in the math libraries that we need that could speed up the calculations

We will know this week how things are going and will continually meet with Adamari to see progress.

title: AR Math Visualizer (Summer 2025 Pilot) - Aug 11
text:

I'm going to be reaching out to the makers of CalcPlot3D to see if they would be willing to talk with Adamari for a solution. Their product is able to render thousands of plot points without a huge load time. It's a bit of a long shot but we will try to see if this could be an avenue for help.

title: AR Math Visualizer (Spring 2025 Pilot) - Mar 31
text:
Summary
We met with Brian and Pavel on Tuesday and had a productive session. Pavel had tested the application and had several suggestions that Brian agreed with. The prioritized items that they would like to see are the following:

    Priorities for new features:
        -Plotting Space Curves of the form r(t) = <x(t),y(t),z(t)>
        -Plotting Vector Fields <x,y,z>=<f(x,y,z),g(x,y,z),h(x,y,z)>
        -Plotting both of them on the same "display"

The plan is to roll out a pilot in summer for the Calc 3 course. We can plan for surveys and potentially A/B testing. Bigger release of this would be in the fall semester. 

Adamari also fixed some of the bugs and implemented custom parsing. 

Will continue to work with Adamari to figure out the possibilities of the new features being implemented in the coming weeks.

I've marked this as needs discussion because due to the heightened political environment two things have happened:

    When Steve went down on his trip to El Paso he was not able to get anywhere close to a detention center. He said that his contacts in El Paso that normally could go into the centers are not able to get in there now. We don't know when we would be able to get this footage.
    Eva is not able to get any footage on her upcoming Mexico trip due to security concerns

What we can do is go through the footage that we have and see what could work in the experience. Eva mentioned she can have some grad students help go through the footage as well during the fall semester if needed.

Eva gets back in mid-July and we can probably regroup then with the plan on how to move forward with the project.

She takes the Panama trip and then comes back for a week and then goes to Mexico and then when she's back it's basically the semester start. This project has always been a bit nebulous but it's been hard to plan everything when the faculty aren't telling us all of their plans.

Regardless - we will receive a dump of the Panama trip footage on Jun 12 when Eva delivers the camera to us. We will upload the content and then give the camera back so she can use it in Mexico.

text:
Updates
I met with Kylie and Michelle to reconvene and finalize production dates and timelines for this project. Their team will be responsible for capturing the phone screeners and any virtual check-ins or interviews through May. 

One of their video needs (a general orientation) has transformed into a scripted onboarding video - we will find a time this week or next week (I've sent them a few options) to bring them to the studio to film that as a screencast, with a hard deadline of March 31st. 

Finally, I've set up a meeting with the two of them on Tuesday to answer any lingering questions and lock down production dates. 

Types of Assets and Timelines
    Phone Screeners: footage filmed/uploaded to Drive by FINs team. 
    General Orientation: scripted onboarding video, needs the video done by March 31st, so production would have to happen next week or two. Studio space.
    App Orientation: capturing presentation/interactions with participants. Week of April 7th (Matthew will be in Vegas, will confer with Alexx and Derrick on their availabilities). 
    Weekly Check-Ins: screen recordings, sent to us. 
    Exit Interview: happening in May. May be either a solo interview or a focus grop, more to come.


Next Steps
    Matthew to meet with Kylie and Michelle on Tuesday regarding upcoming production. 
    Matthew to onboard Alexx and Derrick on this project, gauge availabilities, and answer any questions they may have.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Responsible and Ethical AI - Dec 11
text:
Summary
    The average course grade has risen from 79.4 to 83.6. This is a result of students who were failing dropping as the course comes to an end.
    The NetID sign up and transcript process seems to have gone smoothly enough. The Registrar's Office was able to share quick feedback with student errors they identified and Mary and Bri made sure the scholars and CTs got the information quickly to problem solve.
    Valya held the last of the 12 professor pop up sessions.
    There was a celebration for the TFs on Friday and a NEON-wide graduation ceremony on Thursday evening—though the students still have today (or maybe until Wednesday) to turn in late assignments.


Next steps
    Student grades are due by 12/22. We'll receive the data on them and much more from the surveys in January and will add that to the final report that will be used to help get ND leadership approval to maintain and maybe expand our offerings with NEON.
    Valya will be submitting an application today for additional funding for Lucy to support this program.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Responsible and Ethical AI - Dec 3
text:
Summary
    Average course grade dipped just below 80% to 79.4%.
    Valya participated in three of the remaining four Professor Pop-Up meetings. The feedback on the one that occurred before this was composed was that it was "great."
    The NetID and transcript process mostly went off without a hitch. Some students forgot to hit the "hold for grades" tab, which meant that they were immediately sent blank transcripts and will have to re-request. Bri and Mary jumped into immediate action to remind the Co-Teachers and students (and TFs) about this step.
        There was a second issue that we also tracked down to students having indicated that they were not enrolled in the course.
    End-of-course surveys went out to students, TFs, and CTs.
    This is the last full week of the course.
    Valya has an opportunity to get NSF report for future implementations of this course (See more in the NEON update).


Next steps
    Surveys went out to Valya, Mary, and Annie and a retrospective is scheduled for next week.
    Lawrence has a draft Final Report, though the key part—on results and impact—will need to wait for the survey data that NEON will share in January.
    NEON will schedule a follow-up/reflection meeting for January.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Responsible and Ethical AI - Nov 26
text:
Summary
    Adjustments were made to Week 12 to adjust for the holiday.
    The average student score dropped from 82.19 to 80.1.
    The Registrar's Office and Pre-College got everything set for the students to receive their NetIDs and then put in transcript requests. So far, no complications.


Next steps
    Valya will be doing four more professor pop-in sessions. this week, meaning she will have connected with each of the 12 schools. We've heard only positive feedback from all sides on these so far.
    The various end-of-course surveys will go out this week.
    Just two weeks left in the course!

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Responsible and Ethical AI - Nov 19
text:
Summary
    Valya did 15-minute Professor Pop-In sessions at the schools last week. She seemed very pleased with the ones about which she reported—though there was one for which she was just left in the Zoom waiting room!
    The NEON team continues to send an update letter the day before our weekly meeting answering any questions we've asked and providing additional updates.
    The average course score jumped two points this past week to 82.19, as the TFs and CTs have focused on getting students to turn in outstanding assignments.
    NEON shared an updated roster list with us that we shared with Pre-College and the Registrar's Office so that everything is in place for the students to begin to access their NetIDs so they can arrange to receive free transcripts.
    Valya identified videos that can be made optional for Week 13, and adjustments have been made to the T-giving week plans.


Next steps
    The end-of-course surveys will go out on 12/1 with a due date (as an assignment for the students) of 12/12—though they can submit responses until 12/22.
    The course wraps up on 12/15.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Responsible and Ethical AI - Nov 13
text:
Summary
    The NEON team shared how valuable the visit to King College Prep was for everyone.
    Lawrence was connected to officials from KCP to connect them to ND Admissions, but the KCP have not followed up yet.
    This week is Week 11 of the course, and Valya will be making 15-minute Professor Pop-Up visits into each of the classrooms.
    The average student score is now 80.9—down just a drop from last week.
    We resolved a Canvas-related issue related to assignments that had been graded being labeled as ungraded.
    NEON is hosting a prep session for Co-Teachers on Thursday to prepare for next week's NetID sign up.
    Sonia, Bri, and Lawrence met to discuss the less than ideal survey instruments that are part of the program this year. We shared a couple of questions with NEON that we would like added to the student end-of-course surveys.
    At an internal working session last Thursday, Bri and Mary made final tweaks to Week 13 and 14 content and put in some additional guardrails for the NetID sign-up process.
    Lawrence met with OIT folks and Dawn to be sure everyone was aligned with how the student NetIDs will be stored and shared, and then synced all of that with the NEON team.



Next steps
    Looking forward to hearing about Valya's sessions with the students and to a smooth NetID rollout!
    We'll continue to follow up on the KCP-Admissions connection and gathering data via the surveys.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Responsible and Ethical AI - Nov 6
text:
Summary
    The NEON leadership team continues to follow through on action items and share helpful updates for our weekly meetings. This week there was specific follow through on challenges for a couple of students at the Newark school.
    Valya is preparing talking notes/script for her Professor Pop Ups, which would take place the week of 11/17.
    Incentives were established for the end-of-course survey, and we're looking for ways to piggy back on that to gather additional data for a post-course report.
    The average course grade is now 81.32, which is down around .5 from the week before.
    We've tried to set up some systems for clarity and consistency on communication between stakeholders and late assignment policies. For all of this, we'll be better prepared next year.
    Bri and the Lucy team will have visited KCP on Friday, and Bri can share highlights with us.


Next steps
    Week 10 of the course is this week, with Valya participating in Pop Up sessions next week.
    Students will be obtaining their NetIDs the week after that, and we're coordinating with NEON and the Registrar's Office to ensure that goes as smoothly as possible.
    We have a working session set for 11/13 to finalize content for Weeks 13 and 14—the last two of the course!

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Responsible and Ethical AI - Oct 31
text:
Summary
    As we had the week before, we received an update with important information and details of follow up on action items from the NEON leadership team the day before we met with them. Destiny, our course success manager, will not be joining our meetings going forward.
    Against our strong recommendation, Valya would like to do professor pop ups in as many NEON classrooms as possible. Mary is arranging that schedule.
    Amaya (TF), Annie, Bri, Mary, and Valya are all set for their visit to KCP on Friday!
    The average grade in the course went up from 79.43 to 81.98 over the past week.
    Students submitting work late and TFs not strictly following the policy as stated in the syllabus for that continues to be a problem. 
    We had an internal team working session on Thursday afternoon to discuss the issue above, make tweaks to some of the remaining course content, plan for Valya’s professor pop ups, and integrate the NEON end-of-course survey as an extra credit assignment in Canvas to encourage participation.


Next steps
    The ND team heads to King College Prep in Chicago on Friday to meet with the NEON scholars and school officials.
    Students will do a Canvas assignment in a couple of weeks to get their NetIDs.
    We’ll be coordinating with NEON and the Registrar’s Office to make sure that goes smoothly, as well as the transcript process after that.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Responsible and Ethical AI - Oct 24
text:
Summary
    We had a very positive and productive meeting with the NEON team this week, though Destiny, our usual contact person wasn't there. Hmmm?
    We settled on a strategy of trying to convince Valya of trying to do just one or no professor pop up sessions given that she's already going to visit one school in person and there is not a lot of time or capacity (for anyone) for more than that.
    We finalized most of the plans for the visit to King College Prep visit on 11/7.
    NEON shared some data on student performance so far. The average course grade is 79.43%, which is inline with most of their other courses.
    There seemed to be good follow up this week in terms of information sharing regarding a new Co-Teacher at one school and some clarifications about when the course was being offered at another school.
    Some of the TFs seem to have fallen behind in grading assignments, and it will be important to follow up on that from our end.
    We discussed the complication of TFs grading the scholars' work and how we might improve the rubric and process for that to ensure comfort and consistency.
    The SWAG was sent to all of the schools.


Next steps
When we meet this week, we will try to:
    Reach agreement on if Valya will do any pop ups and, if so, the details.
    We'll make our plans for the 11/7 visit to KCP.
    Make content adjustments for Thanksgiving Week.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Responsible and Ethical AI - Oct 16
text:
Summary
    Complications at several schools were discussed
        Moreno Valley–no students. Still waiting for follow though by NEON to close the course.
        Westland Hialeah: Co-Teacher left, no new CT assigned yet.
        Newark: No CT and the class is held after school Still no follow through by NEON.
    In general, the NEON team seemed adversarial at this meeting. We met with the NEON leadership team yet again on this and tried to emphasize that the problem is not a two-sided communication issue but a one-sided problem with lying, withholding information, failing to focus on the outcomes for the students and TFs.
    Recorded Teaching Fellow session this week, as ND is on fall break.
    We discussed possibilities for engaging with students at the King College Prep visit in Chicago for 11/7, but still no final word from NEON on the schedule for the day.
    Valya indicated that she would still like to do a brief pop-up appearance in each of the classrooms and is working on the schedule for that.
    We held a working session to fine tune details for Weeks 9 and 10.


Next steps
    NEON said they'd get back to us within 24 hours about how to address our ongoing problems with Destiny.
    We'll have our next working session on 10/30 to fine tune Weeks 11 and 12.

title: Responsible and Ethical AI - Oct 9
text:
Summary
    Annie has continued to onboard, Mary, the new Lead Teaching Fellow.
    Annie and Valya were away this week, so we did an email check in.
    One of the schools continued to have no students show up, and we've begun the process for dropping the course there.
    The SWAG is ready to go to the schools; just waiting on the joint letterhead (NDL and Lucy) to be finished and Valya's signature to be secured for the letter.
    The team is meeting this week, primarily to focus on content for Week 7 when the Teaching Fellows will be on Fall Break and to prep for the 11/7 visit to King College Prep.


Next steps

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Responsible and Ethical AI - Oct 2
text:
Summary
    We welcomed Mary Wachira to the project as the new Lead Teaching Fellow. Annie will be with us two more weeks as she onboards Mary.
    Schools worked through Week 4 last week, and our working group finalized/revised details on Weeks 5, 6, and 8 on Canvas. 
    Week 7 will be during fall break. One Teaching Fellow will create a recording to be used at the usual TF sessions that week.
    We continue to have communication and information-sharing issues with Destiny—the course success manager from our partner organization, which is now called NEON (National Education Opportunity Network).
    Friday marked the last day that students can sign up for the course.


Next steps
    Valya and Annie will be away this week, but we will meet and then have a working session on 10/15, as we continue to finalize and revise Canvas details based on how things are going in the course.
    Amaya, the TF at King College Prep, will join us as we plan a team visit there for 11/7.
    Lawrence will mail out SWAG as soon as we get the final roster numbers for each school.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Responsible and Ethical AI - Sep 25
text:
Summary
    Week 3 of the course seemed to pass with less drama.
    Valya and Annie were away at a conference, so there was no team meeting.
    We just learned that Annie will be shifted to another project and that Mary Wachira will become the new Lead Teaching Fellow.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Status update - Mar 14
text:
Summary
We had an informative meeting with the NEEL team on Monday and got greater clarity on both the various roles (Teaching Fellow, Lead Teaching Fellow, etc.) but the way that the content is delivered and learned in the high school classrooms.

For our course, there will be approximately 10 classrooms around the country of roughly 25 students each.

Each class meets for three hours each week (14-week course) with most, if not all, of the media content involving straightforward teacher lectures of less than 10 minutes. NEEL indicated that there is no set amount of media content for each week and that it might range anywhere from one to several lecture videos/week, depending on the content and what else the students need to do.

Next steps
We'll be meeting on a weekly basis starting—we hope—this week to get the learning content together with production kicking off in the late spring. A NEEL coordinator will be joining for most of our meeting sessions to provide insight and guidance on the learning content and approach.

Everything seems inline for the iTREDS Scholars to serve as the Teaching Fellows (one for each classroom), and we're trying to determine who will serve as the Lead Teaching Fellow.

Nathaniel and Roberto still wanted to pursue making the course credit-bearing, but we are helping them to realize/accept that this just won't be feasible. Other options are for it to go on the Comprehensive Learner Record, be a requirement for incoming students, and/or be integrated in some way (dorm challenge) into campus life.
        (Update: Sonia helped Nathaniel accept that this will not be a credit-bearing course.)

    Decided also to connect with Ron and Erin also regarding outreach to campus powerbrokers and stakeholders for transparency/inclusion as well as buy in and support.

project_name: AI for the Notre Dame Community
author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: AI Literacy - Apr 17
text:
Summary
    We held a focus group with four or five students on Monday. The big insights/takeaways were 
        The primary drivers for student engagement with a course like this would be if it were credit-bearing and gaining skills they can use in school/work, as well as curiosity about how AI works.
        Students would also want it to be linked to some incentive related to student campus life (e.g. dorm challenge).
        Students would be suspicious that the University would want to be tracking their AI use and/or trying to catch them cheating.
            Need to change the culture/attitude felt by students regarding the University and AI.
        Students are very interested in how AI will impact them on the job and in society in general, how to spot misinformation, and how to write effective prompts.

    Main takeaway for the team was the need to meet with key campus stakeholders to get buy in on moving forward with what we've learned. Meeting is set with Ron and Erin Klawitter (Asst. VP Undergraduate Education) for this afternoon (4/17).



Next steps
    Based on insights from focus group (and other research) and takeaways from the meeting with Ron and Erin, we will return to Yi's project timeline and course map/content development.

    In terms of AI statements and acknowledgements, here is a sample AI-generated image that highlights that it is “photorealistic” and then states directly at the end that it has been (Generated with assistance of AI).
    

https://app.asana.com/app/asana/-/get_asset?asset_id=1215924786396976

        This seems like a solid approach to take across the SpectrumX courses for images, unless ODL has a recommendation otherwise.
    Nick has proposed the wording below to use at the end of each lesson in the course. Again, this and appropriately adapted versions seems like a good approach to use across the SpectrumX courses, unless ODL has a recommendation otherwise.

https://app.asana.com/app/asana/-/get_asset?asset_id=1215924786396978


    It is Lawrence’s understanding that ODL/Kuangchen will develop language for an overall AI transparency statement for inclusion in M1 of Nick’s (and the other courses) that might be based off the example that Sonia shared last week:
(Generative AI Disclosure Statement
This document was developed with support from Claude to assist in content feedback and refinement. All AI-generated contributions were reviewed, edited, and adapted to align with the document's goals and intended tone. Final decisions about structure, messaging, and content were made by human authors to align with pedagogical intent and support responsible AI use across diverse learning contexts and audiences. For inquiries, permissions, or collaboration requests, contact: info@aiforeducation.io
Website: https://www.aiforeducation.io/

    Finally, to clarify a question raised last week, SpectrumX intends to launch all three courses independently and without marketing as they are completed in September and October. Down the road from that soft launch–with lessons learned from the soft launch (Such as–from which countries are the students coming from)--SpectrumX will do a marketed and coordinated announcement about all of the courses.

----------------------------------------

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jun 14
text:
Summary

    Kuangchen provided Nick with feedback on his all-but-finalized M3.L4 content. There was some cleaning up of mentions of other lessons/content learned that were cleaned up to keep the content evergreen and spelling out acronyms each time they appeared for the first time in a lesson.
    Kuangchen thought Nick was especially effective in ensuring that the "Elaboration" sections were engaging and focused on connections to the students' day-to-day lives.
    Update: Nick is recommending that the three new courses (Nick's, David Reed's and Tom Hazlett's) be launched without fanfare independently as they are ready to go up in Sept/Oct with a coordinated and well-marketed announcement of the entire Radio Spectrum Coursera series to follow that after the classes have established themselves. Lawrence is bringing this approach to the SpectrumX team, but Nick would like ODL's feedback/sign off.
    Question(s) 1: Nick produced images using Gemini to directly match the relevant content in the M3.L4 and M3.L3 lessons. The captions all begin with language along the lines of, "Figure 1: Photorealistic aerial view of . . ." Is ODL good with using that type of language to indicate it is not an actual photograph? For source citation, does ODL have language that it uses/recommends for AI-generated content? Can there be one statement in the beginning along the lines of, "Photorealistic images in the learning content were all produced by Prof. Laneman using Gemini XYZ (whatever the specific tool is)"?
    Question 2: Does ODL have AI Statement language it recommends/wants Nick to use in M1 to indicate that learning content was, at times, drafted and refined through Prof. Laneman prompting Claude XYZ (whatever specific tool/version) with Prof. Laneman finalizing all content himself? Or should we just turn the language above into that statement.

----------------------------------------

Next steps

    Nick's goal is to integrate Kuangchen's feedback on M3.L4 this week as well as all but finalize the M3.L3 content and M3 HW this week to share with Kuangchen upon his return next Monday.
    We would then try to get Kuangchen's feedback to Nick in advance of the the Thursday, 6/25 meeting, so we can finalize and pass off all of the content prior to Nick leaving for London on 6/29.
    Lawrence's understanding is that ODL will—consulting with Nick as needed with assistance from Lawrence—build the course on Coursera over the summer witha targeted launch by October 1, if not earlier.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jun 8
text:
Summary

    For everyone with a betting interest on the line about which of these things would happen last: the end of life as we know it on Earth, the Knicks winning an NBA title, or Nick finishing his Module 3 course, it looks as if the Knick and Nick doubters are about to lose some Donut Dollars!
    Nick has completed his review and revision of the Claude-drafted M3.L4 content and produced what look like (to a definite non-expert/G$) great images using Gemini.
    Time permitting, Kuangchen will do a final review of this content and provide Nick with feedback this week. Nick will use any insights from Kuangchen to review and revise the M3.L3 content (currently in Claude draft), which will leave only the M3 homework assignment as the last step for Nick.
    For anyone interested in the source of the breakthrough for Nick, it was two-fold: He has to teach this content over the summer in London, and his M2 course on Coursera has been very-well received with 260 students having completed the course so far this year (The goal was 100!). Nick took that as validation for his approach and refined his Claude prompts by directing the AI to mimic the tone, style, and length of the M2 material, which has dramatically cut down on his need for additional editing.
    It will be interesting to see what the team thinks of the images Nick produced using Gemini, which could impact ODL's need for/use of stock images going forward.


----------------------------------------

Next steps

    If feasible, Kuangchen will review Nick's material and provide him with feedback before the end of the week.
    Nick can then make any final changes to M3.L4 and final draft M3.L3 while Kuangchen is out.
    Nick's goal is to have everything completed and passed on to ODL for the course build before he leaves on 6/26 for London.
    Shout out to Kuangchen for all of his work on the M2 course, which—with no marketing at all—has a total enrollment of 545 on Coursera, with 429 students starting the course, and 250 completing it in less than six months!
    Let's go K/Nicks!

    Lawrence is trying to arrange a time to meet with Nick to get answers to a few questions related to the M2 Coursera course, including a couple of student comments/questions that need an informed response. 
    Once Lawrence can connect with Nick, he can try to get Nick to calendar a little time for the M3 content.
    In terms of the M2 course, the updated numbers (change from the previous week in parentheses). are
    Unique visitors: 1,012 (+95)
    Total enrollment: 178 (+18)
    Current enrollment: 169 (+16)
    Started course: 101 (+14)
    Completed course: 6 (+8)


Next steps
    As noted above, two big tasks for Nick are
        Finalizing the M3 course content.
        Addressing a few issues related to the M2 course.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Feb 22
text:
Summary
    Trigger Warning: Individuals vulnerable to adverse reactions from surprising news are encouraged to read no further.
        Nick has drafted all of the M3.L4 material!
    So the M3 course, Terrestrial Wireless Networks, is almost now fully drafted. 
        Some of the L3 material and all of the L4 material await revision. And the homework (which becomes M5) remains. But progress is progress!
    In terms of the M2 course, Point-to-Point Wireless Telecommunications, that was completed and launched in December, we are up to:
        Unique visitors: 917
        Total enrollment: 160
        Current enrollment: 153
        Started course: 101
        Completed course: 6
        Though we don't have anything against which to benchmark, these seem like strong numbers for a course that has not been actively marketed yet.
        All of the (limited) feedback has been positive so far. There have been a few student suggestions for information that seems incorrect to them (mostly in terms of Knowledge Check responses or data) that have been forwarded to Nick for review.


Next steps
    The job now is to prompt and support Nick to review, revise, and finalize the M3 and M4 course content (and HW) so the course can be built and launched.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Feb 15
text:
Summary
    This project is on track in the sense that Anja from SpectrumX indicated that she would be telling Nick last week, that if he did not have the M3 content completed this week, she was taking the project away from him and having someone else complete it. 
    That track seems more likely to get to the next station sooner than Nick will get it there. However, it's not particularly likely that Anja will follow through on conveying that message to Nick and/or following through with someone else.
    In the meantime, Lawrence continues his outreach to and prodding of Nick to close the loop on M3, an effort that has not proven effective.


Next steps

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Feb 6
text:
Summary
    The 'missing' video was located, so that is complete and there is just the M3 HW, Lesson 3 revisions, and Lesson 4 content to be completed.
    Unfortunately, as of Friday afternoon, Nick had not made progress on those.


Next steps
    Lawrence and Nick are scheduled to meet today (Monday) on the remaining M3 content.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jan 30
text:
Summary
    No response to email and text outreach to Nick about setting up working sessions to complete the M3 material, and no new content uploaded to GitHub. Nick did accept a meeting with Lawrence today.
    In terms of the M2 course on Coursera, the latest numbers are
        612 visitors
        104 enrollments
        98 currently enrolled
        65 started course
        3 completed the course
        3 content likes (no dislikes)



Next steps
    Lawrence will attempt to get Nick through the remaining M3 content before next Monday.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jan 23
text:
Summary
    "On track" might be a bit of a euphemism for "still trying to get Nick to finish his work."
    Despite the limited detail provided by Nick, Derrick successfully completed and shared his image search for M3.L4.
    Kuangchen has the M3 course build framework complete on Coursera and just needs the remaining content.
    Rather than meeting with Nick, we will respond to work he completes and shares, reviewing, editing, and uploading as appropriate.
    The NSF review was last week, and they were very pleased with the launch of the M2 course and the development so far of M3.
    Nick's M2 course—with just a cold launch on 12/24—has 453 unique visitors, 74 people who have enrolled in the course, 42 who have started the course, and three who have completed it. Though limited, all of the student content ratings have been thumbs up so far.



Next steps
    Nick is to complete the remaining content by 2/13, and our goal is to have the course build complete and ready for Coursera QA by 2/27.
    SpectrumX will be meeting about the timing for the rollout and marketing of the course.
    SpectrumX will be meeting with Nick with the goal of getting him to agree to someone else serving as the SME for the M4 course. SpectrumX will take responsibility for getting that developed.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jan 18
text:
Summary
    Nick did no additional work on the remaining M3 material, as he was focused on the NSF review on 1/20.
    Kuangchen built out a private version of the M3 course that Nick can show to the NSF reviewers on 1/20.
    Nick provided direction for Derrick for image gathering for M3.L4.
    Kuangchen identified that if Nick wants the M3 course to match the M2 course, he will need to make a short closing video for M3.L4.
    As of 1/19/26, the M2 Course has had 377 unique visitors, 61 enrollees, 37 people starting the course, two people finishing the course, and three thumbs up on content.


Next steps
    Nick is hoping that down the road ODL can review and upload the M3 material when he finishes it so it's consistent with the other material rather than having the Clemson team do that.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jan 9
text:
Summary
    Nick did not get to any additional content development over the break. 
    Kuangchen will start the M3 course build on Coursera with the material that's been developed so far. We told Nick that we can add anything that he shares by next Thursday in advance of the NSF review on 1/20.
    32 people (as of Friday at noon)—presumably random students—have signed up so far for the M2 course that was launched on 12/24! There are three thumbs up on content, no thumbs down, and one student comment that is in an Asian language.
    Nick has asked for help searching for images for M3.L4. Either Derrick will provide that assistance or we'll let Nick know what websites he can check where we can get images for him that he identifies.


Next steps
    The goal is to get as much of the M3 content up on Coursera for the 1/20 NSF review.
    We will meet with Nick through the end of the month to support content development, and then the full SpectrumX project will be turned over to a team from Clemson.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Dec 18
text:
Summary
    Nick was late with completing his final screencast (M3 Conclusion), but Kevin graciously completed the edit prior to the Christmas break.
    Kuangchen shepherded the course through the publishing and launching processes on Coursera, but we did not hear back from Coursera that the review process was finished before the Friday, 12/19 deadline. However, SpectrumX did not finish preparing its preliminary materials for NSF review by then either. Ultimately, Kuangchen made Emily from SpectrumX a student in the private version of the course and Emily shared her sign-in credentials in the NSF preliminary materials for anyone who wanted to check out the M2 course.
    Coursera finally completed the review on 12/24, and the course is now available publicly.
    Lawrence checked in with Nick repeatedly over the break to see if he was making the planned progress on the M3 content. There was no response from Nick and no sign of content development on the GitHub repository.


Next steps
    If Nick somehow did complete the planned content, he will be in a position to work with Kuangchen in January to finalize M3 for publication on Coursera. We've set a meeting for Thursday to check in with Nick.
    NSF will be doing a full review of the SpectrumX content on January 20.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Dec 15
text:
Summary

Next steps

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Dec 11
text:
Summary
    As of 2:23 PM on Thursday, Nick had not passed on his review and revision of the course description and module learning objectives language that Kuangchen needs to finish building M2 as a standalone course on coursera.
    Nick also has one screencast due to given Friday (12/12) morning and a final screencast due on 12/16.


Next steps
    The goal is/was to have M2 published on Coursera as a course before break so that SpectrumX can reference it in their "read ahead" materials being shared with NSF before Christmas for feedback in advance of the 1/20 NSF check in.
    Lawrence is coordinating with the SX stakeholders to help prepare overall for the Education and Workforce Development Group's materials for sharing with NSF, which will include, if Nick does his work, the drafted learning content for Module 3 as another potential standalone course on Coursera.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Dec 3
text:
Summary
    The project is on track because we switched tracks: Sonia and Lawrence met with Nick and reached agreement that the focus now will be to get Module 2 published as a course on Coursera so that NSF can see a finished product and so we can gather some insight from student experience of the content.
    We'll also try to get the M3 material ready so that it can be published down the road.
    Basically, Nick and Kevin will finish the M3.L4 screencasts in December while Kuangchen does the M2 course build on Coursera. 
    Nick will then need to catch up some on content revision during the break and work with Kuangchen in January to develop the M3.L4 content.


Next steps
    Lawrence will coordinate with the SpectrumX team to have preliminary materials ready to handoff to NSF before Christmas Break. They'll provide feedback and then we'll prepare the materials for the NSF check in on January 20.
    Lawrence will coordinate with Stephanie, the SpectrumX communications person, on marketing the rollout of the M2 course.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Nov 26
text:
Summary
    Nick has kept up with the screencast development.
    In theory, this past week was to give him time to catch up on the learning content revision.
    Sonia, Kuangchen, and Lawrence have met to put together a plan for Nick to publish the current Module 2 and—if he can complete the outstanding work—Module 3 as standalone courses on Coursera.


Next steps
    We will meet tomorrow with Nick to share that plan and come to alignment on how the remaining time working with him will be spent.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Nov 21
text:
Summary
    Nick continues to keep up with the screencasts but is also falling farther behind on the content development.
    He completed and shared his recording of the M3.L3 conclusion screencast but has not yet updated the L3.1-3 lessons or the L4s. He indicated that he'd try to catch up over the weekend.
    Kevin has (I believe) completed the edits to the M3.L3 conclusion.


Next steps
    Sonia and I will, if he's available, try to connect directly with Nick soon after Thanksgiving to get alignment and understanding on what the handoffs will be before Winter Break and what's feasible and appropriate in terms of support for whatever comes next for Nick's project.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Nov 13
text:
Summary
    Nick kept pace with his screencast work—passing the M3.L3 Overview screencast on to Kevin and drafting the Conclusion screencast.
    He was less successful catching up on the learning content, making decent progress on M3.L3.1 but not finishing, with M3.L2-3 still in draft form.
    Kuangchen provided Nick with a way to both stimulate student engagement and reduce the abundance of content in the draft lessons.


Next steps
    We set up the priorities for Nick's work in advance of next week, with a focus on keeping up with the screencasts and catching up on the content finalization before drafting the new learning content.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Nov 6
text:
Summary
    Nick did not finish his updates to M3.L3.1-2 last week.
    The L3.1 is partially revised. The L3.2 material is still the AI draft).


Next steps
    Assuming Nick catches up with the work from last week, his tasks for this week include:
        Finalize M3.L3 Overview screencast (which he indicated he'll have done tomorrow)
        Draft M3.L3 Conclusion screencast
        Draft M3.L3.3 content (already done through Claude)

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Oct 31
text:
Summary
    Nick had most of his work for this week completed when we met (One screencast recorded, one drafted, one sub-lesson revised, and three sub-lessons drafted) but did need until Friday to complete revisions to one previously drafted sub-lesson.
    Nick completed the M3.L2 Conclusion screencast and passed that on to Kevin who turned it around quickly for Nick to review.
    Nick's graduate students provided QA feedback on Module 2. Most of it involved typos and minor edits. Nick will integrate those and then pass on the content for Kuangchen to upload to Coursera.
    In general, the content Nick has been producing is way too detailed and technical. Kuangchen and Lawrence provided feedback on how to both cut the content and integrate learner activities and assessment to promote student motivation and engagement. It's unlikely that Nick will be able to integrate those ideas/suggestion in the content during our remaining few weeks.


Next steps
    Nick has a somewhat lighter workload this week as he is traveling. We'll probably just do email check ins. 
    Nick's work for this week involves revising a lot of AI-assisted content (for M3.L3.1-2). Ideally, he will use the approach Kuangchen has developed for that. We'll see.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Oct 24
text:
Summary
    Nick completed most of the work on time for this week and finished up immediately after our meeting. There was one screencast on which to provide feedback and one to produce. There was also old content to revise and new content to draft for Module 3, Lesson 2.
    Kuangchen set up a system that should help Nick revise his AI-drafted content a little more quickly or even empower his students to do that.
    And we tried to set up a workflow to keep him on target.


Next steps
    Next week will be a big test, as Nick has a lot of work to complete and is not going in ahead as he was the week before. We'll see.
    Kevin has the M3.L2 Conclusion screencast for editing that we'll pass back to Nick for final review.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Oct 16
text:
Summary
    Nick completed all of his work for this week and a good chunk of next week's work.
    Nick detailed his processes for the content development and organization on GitHub for open-source access and engagement from reviewers/supporters.
    Nick and Kuangchen made some minor review and revisions to M3.L1 work that is now ready for upload to Coursera and set up a plan to review some additional material in preparation for the SpectrumX meeting walkthrough next week.



Next steps
    Nick's progress was very much AI supported, so he has a lot of review and revision work this week to finalize it.
    Nick also has preparations that need to be made for the SpectrumX gathering in Madison.
    Kevin will edit the M3 Overview screencast.
    Kuangchen will provide some review and feedback on the content Nick just developed and/or is preparing for next week
    Nick will prompt his students to get going on the QA.
    Nick has additional work to complete for next week from his M3 Timeline.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Oct 9
text:
Summary
    For one week, at least, Nick met his benchmarks, finalizing all of the M3.L1 content and drafting his M3 Overview screencast.
    Nick also developed some of the SpectrumX overview content that will be part of the tours he provides on the site later in October and early November.
    Kuangchen finished uploading all of the M2 screencasts and will add the M3 videos to the same playlist.
    Kuangchen also updated and added Nick to the Coursera site, but we were not able to add Nick as an instructor. Request is in to Coursera for help, though Sonia may know how/have authority to make that happen.
    Kuangchen also created a private version of the course and added Nick's research team, which will conduct the QA.


Next steps
    For this Thursday, Nick has to record the M3 Overview screencast, draft the next screencast, and draft the learning content for a chunk of the M2.L2s.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Oct 2
text:
Summary
    Kevin shared the M2 FCs. We will be uploading them to the SpectrumX YouTube channel, where they will remain unlisted and then be embedded in the course by Kuangchen.
    Kuangchen is creating a private second version of the course that Nick's research team will use for QA purposes.
    We shared a timeline with Nick for completing M3 before the end of the year. The government shutdown has complicated his timeline and as of the end of the day Thursday, Nick had not determined if it makes sense to move forward at this time on M3.


Next steps
    Depending on Nick's decision, we will move forward to M3 or just help Nick prepare to share M2 with his stakeholders.
    Eventually we will need to prepare to handoff the materials we've developed to whatever team will work with SX next.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Sep 25
text:
Summary
    Kevin and Nick exchanged edits and updates on the last few screencasts.
    Kuangchen integrated the M2 HW and revised the instructions process. He also added a peer review and discussion feature. Nick gave it all a thumbs up.
    The videos will be hosted as "unlisted" on YouTube where they will be accessed for the course.
    Nick has his research team prepared to do the QA.
    Nick developed a plan for 'touring' various levels of SX stakeholders through the module.
    Nick would like to work on M3 through the end of the year.


Next steps
    Once Kevin concludes and shares the videos, Kuangchen will invite Nick and the research team onto Coursera for the QA.
    Nick will coordinate uploading the videos to YouTube with Stephanie.
    The ODL team will determine whether and how to move forward with Nick.
    Nick will prepare M3 on GitHub.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Sep 18
text:
Summary
    Nick put together a sample/example of the Module 2 homework. Kuangchen will integrate that and the instructions into the Coursera site.
    Kuangchen updated the course build with the Lesson 4 content. Looks great—especially with the valid image work by Derrick!
    Nick also completed his M2 Intro screencast. Kevin is working on edits to that and the four L4 screencasts.
    A timeline was shared with Nick for completing all aspects of M2 and preparing the Coursera site for the SpectrumX community and NSF team to tour.
        Nick will have graduate students from his research team conduct the QA.
    A timeline, with strict limits on ODL's involvement, was also shared for work on M3 and M4 to the end of the year. 


Next steps
    Kuangchen will continue the course build while Kevin and Nick work toward Final Cuts.
    Looking to have the course build done by the beginning of October for the QA in advance of a November NDF review.
    We won't be working with Nick beyond 12/19 or the end of October if he chooses not to move forward on M3 and M4.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Sep 11
text:
Summary
    Derrick revised and shared all of the images that Nick needed.
    Kevin completed edits to the M2 screencasts for Nick's review (which he was supposed to complete Friday).
    Nick, belatedly, completed all M2 work outside of making a model/sample version of the HW slide show.
    Kuangchen has started the course build on Coursera and took Nick for a tour.
    We settled on the approach for sharing the Coursera site with stakeholders—taking them on a guided tour rather than publishing publicly for now. 
    Kuangchen is going to create an infographic to help capture and clarify the structure of the module within the greater course context.
    Nick will record the M2 intro as a screencast (by Wednesday)
    Timeline has been set up for M2 to be available to model for NSF in early November.



Next steps
    Nick is to share the M2 Intro screencast he records by Wednesday. Kevin will try to turn that around to share with Nick by 9/23 with final edits slated for 9/26.
    Kuangchen will continue with the course build and migration.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Sep 7
text:
Summary
    Technically, Nick violated the agreement we had that he'd complete all of his Module 2 work by the end of the day Friday. He did not do that.
    At the end of the day Friday, Nick had—with advice and feedback from Kuangchen—drafted the M2 conclusion and homework. But these still needed some images and slides prepared.
    Nick had also—again with insight from Nick—completed the lesson discussion prompts.
    However, Nick had not completed sub-lessons L4.2-4.
    It does look (early afternoon Sunday) as if—with help from images found by Derrick, as if Nick will have those lessons completed sometime today.



Next steps
    Based on the final status of Nick's M2 work, we will determine if, when, and how we'll continue work on Modules 3 and 4.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Aug 29
text:
Summary
     Derrick and Nick established an identifying, naming, and sharing system for the images and icons Derrick has been collecting and producing.
    Nick finalized the draft M2 conclusion and drafted the remaining HW and discussion prompt content (all for Kuangchen's review).
    Lawrence adapted the L4.1-4 lesson scripts on ChatGPT based on Nick's input.


Next steps
    By noon on Tuesday, Nick will produce the L4 content using the scripts from Lawrence and images from Derrick.
    With Kuangchen's feedback, Nick will finalize the M2 conclusion, HW, and discussion prompts by end of day Friday.
    If Nick gets all of that done, we will discuss/consider if and how to produce the next module.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Aug 21
text:
Summary
    Derrick created the icons in PPT, as per Nick's request, and Nick provided feedback for revisions.
    Lawrence produced narrative-style scripts for the remaining L4 sub-lessons. Nick then refined Lawrence's prompt, and Lawrence produced revised versions.
    Nick developed an approach for the L4.1 prompts and M2 homework that he will implement in the coming days.


Next steps
    Derrick and Nick will finalize the images and icons and embed them in the content.
    Nick will make any final revisions to the narrative-style scripts and record those screencasts.
    Nick will finalize the L4.1 prompts and homework for Kuangchen's review upon his return.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Aug 14
text:
Summary
    With a big assist from Kuangchen and Derrick, Nick caught up a good bit, and there's a decent chance that he will have Module 2 complete by the August 31 deadline (with a couple of items that Kuangchen wants to check/revise) on his return.
    Derrick created a frame folder for images and icons he's collecting for feedback from Nick. Derrick will then create PPT versions of the figures and images for Nick to integrate into his materials.
    Kuangchen used ChatGPT to create a prompt that helps generate the more narrative-style scripts needed for the M2L4 sublessons.
    Nick completed the script for the Module 2 overview, as well as the screencasts for M2L4.


Next steps
    We'll use Kuangchen's prompts to draft the remaining L4 sub-lesson scripts.
    Nick will draft and screencast the M2 conclusion.
    Nick will draft the M2L1 Discussion Prompts and brainstorm on the HW assignments that he'll finalize with Kuangchen when he returns.
    Derrick will continue with the image gathering and figure designing.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Aug 10
text:
Summary
    While we set up a feasible plan for Nick to complete the work, it still requires him to follow through in a robust and complete way, and so far, he has not done that.
    Nick followed through to a limited degree on his top two priorities—the M2L4 overview and developing a Lesson 1 template. He did not get to his third priority (the M2L4 conclusion)
    Kuangchen suggested that the L4.1 material would benefit from more of a narrative style (and then Kuangchen volunteered to draft one as a model!).
    Derrick drafted some really great icons to use in the block figures that appear throughout the content.
    Lawrence used Nick's content and his old MVP scripts to develop draft intro scripts for all of the M2 videos/screencasts.


Next steps
    Kuangchen will revise the M2L4.1 script into a more narrative style.
    Nick will (We hope!) draft the T2M2 Overview and Conclusions and record the screencasts, and then do the same with the M2L4 Overview and Conclusion.
    Ideally, Nick will provide feedback for Derrick for any revisions to the icons.
    Nick has three weeks to finish all aspects of M2 (other than the studio recording of the Module Intro video), or the project will not go on.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Aug 3
text:
Summary
    As per Nick's request, Kuangchen provided feedback on the level of difficulty for Module 2, Lesson 3.
        Kuangchen indicated that the lesson was moderately difficult but also provided some approaches or tools that could be shared to make the material more accessible to students.
    Nick shared material produced in conjunction with ChapGPT for Module 2, Lesson 4.
    Nick agreed to splitting up each of the current modules into what will be three courses on Coursera and extending our time for putting that all together through the end of the year.


Next steps
    Derrick will help out identifying images and links to go with the material developed so far in M2L4.
    Nick is working on the M2L4 overview, a template to be used for all of the L1s, and the conclusion for M2L4.
    Nick will ask a grad student for help revising the L4 sub-lessons.
    Kuangchen will support the development of soft skills lessons for Module 0 to help students who find the material challenging.
    Lawrence will draw up a timeline that Nick must meet or work on the project will stop.

author: Kuangchen Hsu
title: Radio Systems Course - Jul 24
text:
Summary
    Built M2.L3.1 to L3.2, including corresponding knowledge checks in Coursera.
    Screencast videos for Lesson 3 are still pending from Nick.
    Verified that the Coursera content is mobile-friendly.


Next steps
By Tuesday, 7/29:
    Nick to complete screencasts for Lesson 3.
    To complete learning content for Lesson 4.
By Thursday, 7/31:
    Nick to complete screencasts for Lesson 4.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jul 17
text:
Summary
    Kuangchen shared the content that was uploaded to Coursera. Nick's comment was, "Those are gorgeous figures."
    Nick did not complete any work for this week. We set up specific tasks to be done by Sunday and Monday. 


Next steps
    We set up specific tasks to be done by Sunday and Monday. If those are completed, we will set specific tasks to be done by next Thursday's meeting. If not, Nick understands that the project is at risk.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jul 10
text:
Summary
    Nick only completed half of his assigned work last week but forewarned us that family and travel would preclude his getting it all done.
    Nick was pleased with the content that Kuangchen uploaded to Coursera. It really did look great!


Next steps
    Nick is shooting to have T2M2L3 completed by tomorrow morning and T2M2L4 for Thursday. This might be feasible given the Lesson 4 content.
    Kuangchen is testing the upload of the table for M2L3.1. Ideally it uploads smoothly to Coursera, if not, we will adjust. Kuangchen, assuming Nick finishes his work, will upload the rest of the Module 2, Lesson 3 material to Coursera.
    Somehow we need to find time in Nick's schedule for him to start scripting the module intro videos and the text to precede the application videos.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jul 4
text:
Summary
    With support from Kuangchen, Nick hit all his marks this week in terms of work that needed to be done, and he seemed genuinely happy and optimistic about the process and banging out the rest of the work. 
    We're still counting on two straight months of follow through from Nick, but, at least, we now have some positive momentum and production.
    For the record, Nick completed all aspects of Topic 2, Module 2, Lesson 2, including the two screencasts. Kevin gave a thumbs up to the one screencast he was able to view before leaving.


Next steps
    Nick's job for this week is to complete T2M2L3.
    He also knows that starting this week or next we have to add two additional work items to his weekly schedule: drafting scripts for the module intro videos and drafting text to lead into the real-world application videos (already fully produced).

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jun 29
text:
Summary
    While Nick has put a good bit of time into setting up a framework for content production that works well for him and has produced some content, we are at the point that he has to actually produce a significant amount of content every week for the next nine to 10 weeks. Failure to do that will put the viability of this project in doubt.


Next steps
    Nick has agreed that all Topic 2, Module 2, Lesson 2 work (including the screencast) will be shared by Tuesday morning and that he will have begun T2M2L2 by Thursday's meeting.
    In a weekend check in, Nick indicated that he had made good progress and was on target for Tuesday's goal.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jun 21
text:
Summary
    Nick produced templates/frameworks for the learning content and refined them with input from Kuangchen.



Next steps
    Nick is to produce all content (including slides and screencasts) for T2M2L2 for this Thursday as a model for the rest of the course.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jun 13
text:
Summary
    Nick gave very positive feedback ("This is amazing!") about Maddie's work on the sample Camtasia screencast editing.
    Module 2, Lessons 2 and 3 learning content are both partially completed


Next steps
    Lawrence, Ted, and Stephanie will be conferring on promotion for the course rollout.
    Kuanchen and Nick will continue to share updates, feedback, and revisions. Goal is for Nick to complete Module 2, Lessons 2-4 by Thursday's meeting.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Radio Systems Course - Jun 6
text:
Summary
    Nick shared slides he has produced and will be sharing them with Kuangchen for an accessibility check.
    We set up a workflow for Nick's content creation using PPT for images (exported to png/jpeg) and Quarto converted to Word documents that Kuangchen will access from GitHub.
    Nick agreed that he would complete the content for Module 2, Lessons 2 and 3 by Thursday.
    Kevin and KC met with Nick at his office for a screencast tutorial. Nick was very pleased with the approach that Kevin and KC want him to take.


Next steps
    Lawrence and Nick will meet Thursday (Kuangchen is out) to confirm that Nick completed the expected work and to determine what gets done for the next week.
    Lawrence will be connecting with Stephanie (the Spectrum X communications person) to make sure we're on the same page with the plans and timing for the rollout in the fall.

author: Kuangchen Hsu
title: Radio Systems Course - May 30
text:
Summary
    Confirmed the list of learning assets and identified how many need to be created: 16 screencast lecture videos, 4 module overview videos, and 34 lecture content pieces.
    The media team will meet with Nick at his office on Wed to review the screencast process.


Next steps

title: Political Science SO Course - Mar 21
text:
Summary
Angela McCarthy, the Department of Political Science, Director of Undergraduate Studies, is putting together a Summer Online course roughly titled Why Political Science?

The course will be a test-case for future iterations and—with the late launch date—we'll point Angela to focusing mostly on synchronous content and very low-production efforts for anything asynchronous.

Political Science is trying to attract more majors and students taking courses in general, as they are getting competition from the Keough Global Affairs major.

Next steps
Kuangchen, KC, and Lawrence will meet with Angela on Thursday to get things going.

text:

Canvas embedding

Maddie is incorporating feedback on a rolling basis and confirmed that there should be no issue adjusting edits accordingly.

Studio onboarding is scheduled for Monday, June 23. Filming will proceed in script order, with R-based scripts saved for the end. Josie will lead recordings; Maddie will provide in-studio support throughout the week.

Summary
We focused on final media delivery, Canvas configuration with TLT, access-model decisions, and readiness for January QA and handoff. Final video cuts were delivered and shared for review, Canvas ExpaND setup was completed (including badge automation), and a solution was identified for view-only access needs. The project remains aligned with the January timeline.
 
Media
    Kevin completed FC, we shared it with Lewis and Fr. Nate for review (Dec 23 deadline).
    Colin began work on the course homepage banner (deadline Jan 8).
Canvas ExpaND & TLT coordination
    Yi continued integrating final feedback for Modules 7-8.
    Confirmed no migration needed (course built directly in Canvas Catalog).
    TLT created a dedicated “Higher-Powered Learning Online Modules” subfolder and copied the course into it.
    Badge automation configured:
        Badge awarded upon completion of Module 8.
        Completion triggers are verified across pages.
    Yi was added as a Designer to the course shell, and invitations were sent to Yi and Lewis.
Access model resolution
    Canvas ExpaND supports an Observer (view-only) role, but it still enforces module prerequisites.
    To meet Lewis’s request for view-only access without prerequisites:
        TLT created a duplicate course titled “Higher-Powered Learning Online Modules -View Only” with completion requirements removed.
        This solution works but introduces additional maintenance overhead if scaled to future courses.
    Recommendation shared with Lewis to use the duplicate course sparingly and consider alternative resource-sharing approaches as HPL migrates more courses from Circle.
Badge Testing & QA
    Badge issuance is enabled for January QA testing.
    Note: test/student accounts without linked emails may not receive badge notifications, but completion logic can still be validated.
Documentation & Handoff
    All assets and correspondence are saved in the HPL project folder.
    HPL Handoff Document finalized, covering:
        Project overview
        Deliverables
        Ownership and maintenance responsibilities
        TLT coordination
    Michael looped in for January coordination and post-handoff discussions with TLT.


Next steps
January Timeline (confirmed)
    Jan 8: Homepage banner delivered (Colin).
    Jan 9: Final Canvas edits and remaining sections complete (Yi).
    Week of Jan 9-16: Full course review in student/admin view; functionality and badge testing (Lewis & Fr. Nate).
    Jan 16: Final QA and approval.
    Week of Jan 19-23: Formal handoff and retrospective.


Open items / Dependencies
    Await confirmation from Lewis/Fr. Nate on FCs.
    Confirm any further needs for view-only access as planning for future courses continues (Canvas ExpaND).
    Complete January QA and formal handoff.

    They had a number of suggestions and requests that all made sense if you squinted a little, and Derrick nimbly integrated them all into his script with little to no complication.
    We've reached out to Steve V to see if he'll play the professor role and to Siiri from FTT to recruit student actors.



Next steps

    Ideally we'll shoot before the end of the month and finalize the videos for handoff prior to Thanksgiving break.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Force for Good Video Project - Oct 2
text:
Summary
    We corresponded with Terry and Erin to try to assure them that we would develop the script for their scenario in a way that would help them reach their course goals but also emphasized that there was not time or need for extensive rewrites (as they had proposed and wanted to propose doing again) given our tight timeline.
    Prior to moving forward on the project, we had to hear from Terry and Erin on the Title VI review by the Office of Institutional Equity (not OGC as I mistakenly indicated last week) about any concerns or issues related to the event that prompted the scenario being used in this project.
    As of the end of day Thursday, we had not heard back from Terry and Erin about their Wednesday meeting with OIE or about our stipulations regarding script development.


Next steps
    We will move forward or not according to what we hear from the McDonnells.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Force for Good Video Project - Sep 25
text:
Summary
    Derrick developed a draft script based on his conversation with the student whose experience will be featured in the scenario.
    Terry and Erin seemed to struggle with how they wanted their learning plans integrated into the script and were asked to make a couple of key decisions that will impact finalization of the script and identification of the actors.


Next steps
    Goal is for Terry and Erin to have made their decisions by last Friday and for Derrick to revise the script in advance of this Thursday's meeting.
    Timeline
        10/9: Pre-production, including finalization of script and identification of actors
        By 10/31: Production (half day of shooting)
        By Thanksgiving: Post production

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Force for Good Video Project - Sep 18
text:
Summary

    Derrick will develop a draft script that we'll share with the McDonnells in advance of Thursday's meeting.


Next steps
    Once the McDonnells sign off on the fundamental details of the scenario, we'll reach out to Siiri in FTT for actors, and Derrick will drive the pre-production forward with a goal of filming in October.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Force for Good Video Project - Sep 14
text:
Summary
    We met with the McDonnells at their offices with the purpose of making clear the connection between the course learning objectives and the video scenario project, as well as preparing for the scenario brainstorm session.
    However, Erin had identified a specific scenario she wanted to use, and so we mostly focused on the development process for that scenario, which will begin with Derrick meeting with the student who will provide background on the experience she had with an American-born professor saying something disparaging about their shared family homeland and society.


Next steps
    We will reconvene after Derrick meets with the student and gets the story basics clarified.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Force for Good Video Project - Sep 7
text:
Summary
    At Sonia's suggestion, KC and Lawrence met with Horane to get insight and suggestions for handling the conversation with students on developing a scenario for the project video.
    Horane's two main suggestions were to not use students from the course and to have the McDonnells clearly articulate the goal for the meeting in advance to the students so that anytime the conversation might veer off track, we can steer it back to the meeting's purpose.
    Lawrence connected with Siiri Scott from FTT, who will help recruit students for the video.
    Lawrence drafted the charter. It's out for review by the McDonnells.


Next steps
    Kuangchen, KC, Derrick, and Lawrence will visit the McDonnells on Thursday to get clarity on how to tie the course goals to the scenario and to prepare for the student scenario development meeting.
    Lawrence will get the charter executed and then develop and share the IP Agreements.

author: Lawrence Greenspun
title: Force for Good Video Project - Aug 29
text:
Summary
    KC and Lawrence met with the McDonnells to revisit the project goal, which involves preparing one case study-style video of a campus-based, student-centered, moral-ethical dilemma.
    Students would be asked to respond to the presented scenario applying tools learned in the course.
    This pilot video would help determine if and how other videos might be developed down the road.
    Derrick was identified as a good match for lead producer on this project, as the McDonnell's are invested in the scenario reflecting genuine student issues and concerns. 


Next steps
    We'll meet with the McDonnells, Derrick, and a learning designer on 9/11 to review the course learning objectives and determine how best to link them to the scenario and surrounding activities, as well as to identify the theme/situation for the scenario.
    The goal is to have the video ready for use in the latter part of the fall semester.

The main goals are to strengthen their applications and to help prepare them for success if they should enter the University (or any university).
    There will be two new veteran-focused modules to start the program.
    The current ASCEND Modules 1, 2, and 3 will become Modules 3 -5, and the current ASCEND Module 4 will be droppted.


Next steps

I talked with KC and Kevin and I will be the one that will initially reach out to the publishers with the information. Once we get details about timelines and price quotes then I'll have Colin drive more of the conversation with the publishers. We have three initial publishers to start with.

We're also looking forward to seeing the exact excerpts that Brett is wanting to use for the Sourcebook content. That will help my conversation with Blake in the OGC.

We will probably use some time in today's meeting to talk more about the game component and see if we can finalize some details there.

author: Michael Lerma
title: Borgo Laudato Si' videos - Jan 5
text:
Summary
Summary of project: 
    4 videos on various topics from ND professors need to be filmed and edited by middle of February
    Topics include theological and environmental connections of sustainability, water quality, alternative energy sources, circular food production and waste
    This is for a course ND students will take at the Borgo for sustainability and faith
    Derrick and Matthew are tagged to take lead on this project
    Michael to set up times with Matthew Kloser and team this week or next week


----------------------------------------


From the Work With Us Request form:

In collaboration with the JTS Initiative (Arun) and Undergraduate Education (Fr. Dan), we are working on a course for Notre Dame students that will take place at the Borgo (Castel Gondolfo - the Pope's Summer Residence) on the intersection of sustainability and faith—a living lab of the encyclical, Laudato Si. The five day long course intended to be taking by ND students and eventually students from Universities across the world, have five 8 hour days of instruction, each with a central theme. We are seeking to tape 4, 8-10 videos by experts that will be a part of each day's focus on that topic. Day 1: The theological and environmental connections of sustainability (Arun and Fr. Dan will do this one); Day 2: Water quality, pollution, etc.; Day 3: Alternative energy sources; Day 4: Circular food production and waste.

We are approaching ND professors to give these short content talks. We are piloting the course with 8 ND students over spring break and then a full class of 20 students will take the course in May after graduation. We would like to have the videos in time for the pilot in March, with the hopes of having videos completed by the middle of February.

Next steps

title: Jim Lang Coursera Course - Jan 26
text:
Summary
We met with Jim the other week and the meeting went well. We clearly outlined the structure we wanted to take with Coursera backed by studies from Coursera that Kuangchen presented on. The course will be 4 modules plus an intro module. The topics will be:

    Bodies
    Spaces
    Time 
    Relationships

In addition, Jim is thinking the video structure will be the following per module:
    One long lecture type video with him as the subject + classroom b-roll as appropriate 
    One short guest appearance video by an ND Learning expert
    One short guest appearance video by another ND faculty member

Jim is aiming to have all of his thoughts on how they want this organized by Feb 9 which will be when we meet next.
